name: Resilience framing versus technical disruption
decision_axis: This lever controls whether the project frames platform-neutral access as an innovation opportunity, a continuity-of-service risk, or a supplier-concentration vulnerability.
consequences: Framing the issue as resilience and procurement risk—rather than privacy ideology or technical disruption—shifts the decision-maker audience from privacy advocates to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the Ministry of Finance, and contingency-planning offices. This increases institutional receptivity but risks diluting the urgency if decision-makers treat the issue as a strategic study rather than a actionable procurement requirement.
options: Anchor the narrative to continuity-of-service failure modes: what happens to tax filing, healthcare access, and banking if Apple or Google changes app-store policies or platform integrity APIs, and how a platform-neutral fallback would restore service continuity.

Emphasize supplier-concentration risk and reduced strategic autonomy: frame Denmark's dependence on two foreign vendors as a procurement governance failure that contradicts the 2026–2029 digital strategy, targeting budget and procurement officials rather than security specialists.

Lead with digital sovereignty and democratic accountability: position platform-neutral access as essential to Danish citizens' right to authenticate without mediating foreign surveillance or commercial data collection, appealing to civil-society organisations and Folketinget's civil-liberties caucus.
review: The resilience frame succeeds at moving the conversation away from ideology, but all three options assume decision-makers will prioritize sovereignty or continuity over the convenience argument: "MitID on iOS/Android works now; why rebuild?" None of the options addresses how to counter the incumbent operator's claim that a fallback path introduces complexity, testing burden, and maintenance cost without proven demand.

name: Demonstrator scope and audience alignment
decision_axis: This lever controls whether the non-production demonstrators target regulatory and procurement specialists, technical architects, or civil-society advocates, and at what level of integration complexity.
consequences: A lean, standards-focused demonstrator (GrapheneOS + FIDO2/WebAuthn + OpenID Connect) is defensible within budget and timeline, but risks being dismissed as a toy that ignores the real complexity of certified identity systems. A deeper demonstrator that simulates eIDAS Substantial-level trust properties increases credibility with architects but stretches technical scope and budget, delaying phase two completion and potentially jeopardizing phase three engagement windows.
options: Build a minimalist proof-of-concept on GrapheneOS that demonstrates FIDO2 and OpenID Connect interoperability with a simulated Danish public-sector IdP, expressly labeling it a feasibility study rather than a system ready for certification, and target regulatory and policy audiences.

Develop a more ambitious simulation that models eIDAS Substantial-level assurance properties—including simulated identity proofing, revocation, audit logging, and platform attestation—to create a credible reference architecture for procurement discussions with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID governance boards.

Pursue a split-path approach: deliver a lightweight mobile demonstrator alongside a hardware-token and browser-based fallback prototype, allowing simultaneous evidence gathering for multiple trust models and reducing the risk that a single technical approach is rejected by different institutional stakeholders.
review: The scope decision directly affects phase two deliverables and phase three credibility, but the options conflate two separable choices: technical depth versus audience targeting. A regulatory audience may actually prefer a lean proof-of-concept if it is accompanied by a clear gap analysis stating what certification work would be required—something none of the options explicitly foregrounds. Budget constraints also make the split-path approach risky if engineering capacity is thin.

name: Institutional engagement sequencing
decision_axis: This lever controls whether the project engages Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, and AltID stakeholders early and iteratively, or reserves direct institutional contact until phase two demonstrators are complete.
consequences: Early engagement (phase one) signals credibility and builds coalition support within decision-making bodies, but risks premature disclosure of findings, incumbent pushback, and dilution of the proposal through compromise before demonstrators provide evidence. Late engagement (phase two onward) allows the project to lead with technical proof rather than policy framing, but sacrifices time to shape AltID procurement requirements and risks arriving after key decisions have hardened.
options: Conduct formal briefings to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, and AltID governance bodies in phase one (months 3–7), sharing the feasibility report and coalition analysis, and explicitly requesting written responses and procurement-planning windows; accept that early disclosure may invite institutional resistance and require coalition pressure.

Pursue informal stakeholder mapping and academic advisory relationships in phase one, then initiate formal engagement in phase two (month 9+) only after demonstrators provide credible technical evidence; assume AltID timeline allows phase three intervention window.

Build phase-one coalition exclusively among civil-society, academic, and EU-level allies; position the project as external technical expertise that informs European Digital Identity Wallet discussions first, then loop back to Danish procurement with EU-harmonized language that is harder for incumbents to resist locally.
review: The timing choice assumes the project can reliably predict AltID's procurement schedule and decision gates, but the plan itself notes that these dates may slip. Engaging too early without technical evidence invites dismissal; engaging too late leaves no room to shape requirements. None of the options explicitly addresses the risk that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen may be sympathetic but lack procurement authority, or that the MitID operator may publicly reject platform-neutral architectures on cost or liability grounds, forcing the project into a prolonged political campaign rather than a technical intervention.

name: Standards body leverage and EU alignment
decision_axis: This lever controls whether the project pursues Danish procurement language independently, seeks alignment with European Digital Identity Wallet framework first, or coordinates simultaneous pressure at both levels.
consequences: Prioritizing EU-level engagement (e.g., through eIDAS technical working groups) creates a "policy diffusion" effect where platform-neutral requirements in European standards make them harder for Denmark to ignore locally. However, EU process timelines are slow, and European requirements may not force Danish implementation for 18–24 months after adoption. Conversely, focusing narrowly on Danish procurement is faster but isolates the project from broader European momentum and leaves Denmark exposed to being overruled if EU-wide digital-identity standards later prioritize platform consolidation.
options: Lead with European Digital Identity Wallet harmonization work: engage relevant EU technical working groups and standards bodies in phase one, build alignment on platform-neutral access language, and use EU consensus as leverage in Danish procurement discussions in phase three.

Prioritize Danish procurement and AltID engagement as the primary intervention point; pursue EU coordination as a secondary avenue only if Danish institutional resistance is strong enough to require European political cover.

Maintain parallel streams: seed EU discussions through academic and standards contacts in phase one (months 1–4), escalate EU engagement in phase two (months 9–16) only if Danish AltID conversations show receptivity, and focus phase three resources on whichever lever—Danish or EU—has demonstrated the most momentum.
review: The parallel-streams approach hedges risk but disperses limited project resources across two institutional arenas with different timelines and decision criteria. EU engagement may enhance credibility but offers no guarantee of European procurement pressure on Denmark before AltID hardens. The options also assume the European Digital Identity Wallet framework will include platform-neutrality language by 2028, which is not assured; if EU requirements remain agnostic on platform architecture, EU alignment becomes a narrative tool rather than a binding lever.

name: Coalition composition and leverage structure
decision_axis: This lever controls whether the coalition prioritizes academic credibility and technical authority, civil-society and privacy advocacy, or procurement-insider expertise and sympathetic government officials.
consequences: A coalition heavy on academic and technical voices commands credibility with architects and standards bodies but may be dismissed by procurement officials as ideological and disconnected from operational reality. A coalition anchored in civil-society organisations and privacy advocates mobilizes political pressure and media attention but risks confirming incumbent operators' framing that platform-neutral access is a special-interest cause rather than a public-sector governance issue. A coalition including procurement officials and sympathetic Folketinget members translates directly to institutional power but requires identifying and securing buy-in from individuals whose institutional position may be precarious.
options: Build a coalition anchored in academic security research, computer-science faculty, and international standards experts (ISO, IETF, W3C); position the project's authority as technical rigor and evidence, targeting architects and conformance assessors.

Anchor the coalition in civil-society organisations (digital-rights groups, privacy advocates, consumer councils) and academic researchers; leverage media and democratic accountability narratives to create political pressure on Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and the Ministry of Finance.

Cultivate a coalition including sympathetic officials from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the Ministry of Finance, and Folketinget's digital policy committee; use insider access to shape procurement language and decision timelines, while reserving academic and civil-society voices for external credibility and public advocacy.
review: All three options assume that coalition composition can be cleanly separated, but the most powerful coalitions operate on multiple levels simultaneously. The risk is that the project may succeed in building one type of coalition early (e.g., academic) and then face credibility gaps with the institutional actors who actually control procurement (e.g., ministry officials). The options also assume sympathetic government officials can be reliably identified and secured without exposing the project to institutional retaliation or the appearance of political capture.

name: Fallback authentication model clarity and regulatory pathway
decision_axis: This lever controls whether the fallback path is defined as a certified eIDAS Substantial-level alternative, a lower-assurance browser-based option, a hardware-token model, or an explicitly transitional bridge pending future standards alignment.
consequences: A certified fallback at eIDAS Substantial level provides maximum institutional legitimacy and regulatory defensibility but requires formal certification work that extends beyond the 24-month project timeline and may introduce cost barriers that lock in the very platform dependencies the project aims to escape. A lower-assurance or hardware-token fallback is faster to implement but will be challenged by regulators and incumbent operators as insufficient for sensitive transactions (e.g., tax filing, healthcare access). A transitional bridge model (explicitly temporary pending EU standards evolution) sidesteps the certification question but risks being treated as a temporary workaround rather than a systemic solution, reducing leverage in procurement discussions.
options: Define the fallback as a full eIDAS Substantial-level alternative pathway requiring formal certification; use the demonstrator to map the certification gap and propose a phased procurement model where suppliers must include fallback-path design in their tenders, with certification completed post-deployment.

Propose a lower-assurance browser-based fallback (eIDAS Low or Substantial for read-only government services, with MFA) that shifts the risk model toward the user and institution rather than the platform; accept regulatory pushback and position the option as a transparent risk-acceptance trade-off versus platform dependence.

Develop a hardware-token (FIDO2 security-key) fallback model that decouples authentication from the OS entirely; position it as a certified, standalone alternative for citizens and organisations willing to adopt hardware tokens, with clear scope limitations (e.g., suitable for government employee authentication, voluntary citizen adoption).
review: The regulatory pathway choice is entangled with the demonstrator scope decision (lever 2), but the options conflate regulatory maturity with technical feasibility. A certified Substantial-level fallback may be feasible technically but regulatory-practice infeasible within 24 months; a lower-assurance fallback is faster but opens the project to the criticism that it is abandoning the very assurance levels that make MitID acceptable for sensitive services. None of the options explicitly addresses the risk that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen may reject any fallback model that does not meet Substantial level, effectively nullifying the leverage the project seeks to create.

name: Project outcome escalation pathway if AltID engagement falters
decision_axis: This lever controls whether the project commits to phase three procurement intervention regardless of AltID receptivity, pivots to publication and standards work, or maintains contingency funding for alternative dissemination and advocacy venues.
consequences: Doubling down on AltID engagement in phase three (even if phase two signals weak institutional interest) preserves the primary objective but risks consuming remaining budget on a low-probability intervention. Pivoting early to publication, EU standards input, and coalition consolidation stretches the project's lifespan and maintains credibility but may appear to stakeholders as failure to achieve the core goal of shaping Danish procurement. Maintaining explicit contingency funding for alternative venues (e.g., publishing an open-source reference implementation, positioning the project as an EU-level standards contribution, or pivoting toward voluntary adoption by public-sector entities) hedges institutional risk but requires transparent acknowledgment that the primary procurement intervention may not succeed.
options: Commit to intensive phase three AltID engagement: allocate contingency budget and extended timeline to iterative stakeholder engagement, regulatory workshop participation, and procurement-process intervention, accepting that this may extend the project beyond month 24 if institutional momentum builds slowly.

Establish a clear decision gate at month 16 (end of phase two): if formal AltID engagement or written procurement interest is not documented, de-scope phase three to publication, EU-standards input, and open-source reference materials; reallocate remaining budget to consolidation and long-term advocacy infrastructure.

Partition phase three funding: reserve 60 percent for AltID engagement, 25 percent for contingency EU-standards and publication work, and 15 percent for alternative dissemination (e.g., open-source implementation, academic spin-out, or civil-society toolkit development) if primary procurement intervention stalls.
review: The decision gate and contingency-partition approaches introduce realism about institutional inertia, but they risk communicating to stakeholders that the project lacks conviction in the AltID pathway, potentially undermining coalition confidence and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen receptivity. The plan's success criteria explicitly require AltID-related language inclusion, not merely publication or EU work; none of the options truly accepts failure to shift Danish procurement—they only manage failure more gracefully. This tension reflects the fundamental asymmetry between the project's ambition (shape procurement) and its actual leverage (technical demonstration and coalition pressure).

name: Internal versus external project staffing and vendor dependencies
decision_axis: This lever controls whether the project builds technical capacity in-house across mobile security, web authentication, and fallback mechanisms, or outsources component development to specialist contractors and consultants.
consequences: In-house staffing creates institutional knowledge retention and reduces vendor dependencies but requires longer hiring lead times, higher fixed costs, and risk of key-person dependencies if turnover occurs during the 24-month timeline. Outsourcing to contractors and consultants accelerates component delivery and reduces fixed overhead but introduces new vendor dependencies, security-vetting overhead, and reduced control over technical decision-making. A hybrid model (core team plus targeted contractor support) is common but requires clear role demarcation to avoid scope creep and cost overruns.
options: Hire a full in-house team covering mobile security, web authentication, regulatory compliance, and policy: prioritize retention, institutional knowledge, and unified technical vision; accept higher monthly burn and longer hiring-to-productivity ramp.

Outsource the majority of technical demonstrator work to vetted security consulting firms and academic contractors; retain a small core team (lead researcher, regulatory specialist, policy coordinator) for strategic direction and institutional engagement; minimize fixed overhead and accelerate component delivery but accept reduced control over technical choices.

Build a hybrid model: hire a permanent core team (lead researcher, regulatory specialist, policy coordinator, and one technical lead) and use selective contractor support for GrapheneOS porting, hardware-token implementation, and security review; ensure at least two in-house engineers understand each major technical component to avoid single points of failure.
review: The staffing decision is often treated as a pure cost-optimization problem, but it directly affects the project's institutional credibility and long-term momentum. Outsourcing heavy technical work may accelerate phase two demonstrators but creates the appearance (and reality) that the project's core technical capability is external, making it harder to sustain engagement in phase three negotiations with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID governance bodies, who will expect the project to own the technical vision. The hybrid model is theoretically optimal but requires clear contract structures and institutional overhead to prevent hybrid-induced scope creep.

name: Demonstrator production use and deployment trajectory
decision_axis: This lever controls whether the demonstrators remain explicitly non-production research artifacts, transition to limited pilot deployments with consenting public-sector entities, or are packaged for voluntary adoption by government agencies.
consequences: Keeping demonstrators as research-only artifacts protects the project from production support and liability burden but risks the appearance of unrealistic toy systems that incumbents dismiss as disconnected from real operational constraints. Limited pilot deployments with sympathetic entities (e.g., one municipal government or university system) provide real-world evidence of feasibility but introduce operational support obligations and create institutional entanglement that may limit the project's ability to criticize pilot results or withdraw if the pilot becomes a political liability. Voluntary adoption (no formal procurement requirement) scales enthusiasm without procurement obligation, but also signals to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen that platform-neutral alternatives lack demand from actual government users.
options: Maintain strict research-artifact status: clearly label demonstrators as non-certified, non-production feasibility studies; resist pilot-deployment requests; focus phase three on standards and procurement language rather than live system operation.

Negotiate a limited, time-bound pilot with one willing municipality or government agency (e.g., a voluntary government employee authentication pilot); use real operational constraints to refine the fallback architecture and gather live evidence of feasibility; accept that the project becomes a service provider for the pilot duration.

Publish the demonstrators as open-source reference implementations and provide lightweight documentation for voluntary adoption by interested government agencies; position the project as infrastructure, not product, and allow demand-driven pilots to emerge without formal project management overhead.
review: The research-artifact approach is safest but risks undermining leverage: procurers often demand to see systems in actual use before committing to requirements. Conversely, committing to a pilot deployment obligates the project to become operational, stretching budget and timeline beyond the stated 24 months and creating the risk that pilot problems are weaponized by opponents to argue that fallback authentication is operationally infeasible. The open-source reference approach is theoretically attractive but assumes the code is sufficiently usable and documented that government agencies will adopt it without formal project support—a risky assumption given Danish procurement culture's preference for contractual accountability.

name: Budget reallocation between phases if milestones slip
decision_axis: This lever controls whether the phase gates are hard (funds held until gate conditions met) or soft (contingency allows phase rollover if intermediate milestones are partially met), and what reallocation rules apply if one phase overruns.
consequences: Hard gates force discipline and create accountability for phase deliverables but risk the entire project if a single phase fails to meet conditions (e.g., if phase one produces no institutional engagement). Soft gates with contingency reallocation provide flexibility and increase the probability of project completion but may encourage scope creep, reduce urgency for timely phase completion, and allow inefficient execution to persist across phases. A hybrid approach—hard gates with a small contingency pool for phase-to-phase bridge funding—balances accountability and resilience.
options: Implement hard phase gates: phase two funding is withheld entirely unless phase one produces all four deliverables (feasibility report, formal proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, fallback concept note, stakeholder matrix); accept that project failure is possible if gates are not met.

Create soft gates with contingency reallocation: reserve 500k DKK (approximately 5 percent of total budget) as a contingency pool that may be reallocated across phases if milestones are at risk; allow phase two to begin at month 8 even if phase one institutional engagement is partial, provided the feasibility report and concept note are complete.

Establish tiered gates: phase two funding (4.0 million DKK) releases in tranches (1.5 million at month 8 if feasibility report is published; 2.5 million at month 10 if institutional engagement letter is received); phase three receives full funding only if both phase one and phase two deliverables are complete by month 16, with deferred phase three activities as phase two contingency.
review: Hard gates create accountability but are rigid in practice; organizations rarely completely withhold funding if 90 percent of work is complete and the remaining 10 percent appears attainable. Soft gates with contingency reallocation introduce moral hazard: teams have weak incentive to prioritize phase completion if bridge funding is available. The tiered-gate approach is more realistic but requires frequent budget re-review cycles and creates uncertainty about available resources, making it harder for the team to plan hiring and contractor engagement. None of the options explicitly addresses what happens if the project consumes 80 percent of phase-one budget on coalition building but produces no institutional engagement—a plausible scenario in a politically resistant environment.

name: Demonstrator feature scope versus stakeholder persuasion investment
decision_axis: This lever controls whether demonstrator effort is concentrated on narrow, defensible technical proof or spread across features designed to appeal to specific institutional audiences.
consequences: Lean demonstrators (FIDO2 on GrapheneOS, browser-based fallback) maximize technical credibility and defensibility at risk of appearing 'too academic' to procurement teams expecting user-experience parity with MitID. Conversely, adding UX polish, multi-factor workflows, or sector-specific integrations (e.g., banking sandbox connections) increases institutional buy-in but dilutes focus and risks scope creep into product-like development. The tradeoff is between proof-of-feasibility and proof-of-institutional-viability.
options: Keep both demonstrators strictly non-production: FIDO2 authentication against a synthetic Danish IdP and a hardware-token fallback path using open standards, with minimal UI refinement, and redirect saved effort toward stakeholder briefings and procurement-language prototyping.

Develop one lean technical demonstrator (FIDO2 on GrapheneOS) and invest the second demonstrator's budget into a higher-fidelity integration scenario (e.g., simulated banking login flow, multi-step authentication) that procurement teams can use for capability assessment without claiming production readiness.

Parallelize a stripped-down technical demonstrator with a separate 'procurement communication package' (interactive documentation, decision-tree visualizations, supplier-neutral architectural diagrams) that translates technical feasibility into language aligned with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's procurement and resilience criteria.
review: Demonstrators are valuable precisely because they are not production systems, yet institutional audiences often conflate non-production proof with feature-poor liability. The real tension is whether effort spent on demonstration depth yields better leverage with procurement bodies than effort spent on translating findings into policy language. None of the options squarely address the risk that even a well-executed demonstrator will be dismissed as a 'research toy' if the institutional narrative around fallback access has not already been anchored in Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's AltID requirements.

name: Browser-based fallback versus hardware-token investment trade
decision_axis: This lever controls whether the non-mobile demonstrator prioritizes web-based authentication (reducing hardware dependencies) or hardware-token workflows (increasing tangible fallback autonomy).
consequences: A browser-based fallback (WebAuthn, OpenID Connect on standard laptops/desktops) offers immediate accessibility and requires no specialized hardware, but still depends on operating-system-level credential storage and may not fully escape mobile-first policy assumptions embedded in MitID's design. A hardware-token approach (FIDO2 USB security keys, smartcard integration) physically separates authentication from device operating systems but introduces supply-chain complexity, cost, and user-adoption friction. Browser-based demonstrates feasibility faster; hardware-token demonstrates supplier independence more convincingly but at higher implementation cost and slower deployment timeline.
options: Develop a WebAuthn-based fallback using standard laptop/desktop browsers with local credential management, paired with a clear architectural note explaining why browser-based fallback remains dependent on OS-level trust, and position it as a 'transition path' while hardware-token requirements mature in European standards bodies.

Implement a hardware-token (FIDO2 USB security key) demonstrator that showcases complete operating-system independence, accept slower development timeline and higher per-unit cost, and use it primarily as a policy artifact to prove that Denmark can establish non-OS-mediated authentication if procurement mandates it.

Develop both in parallel at lean scope: a WebAuthn prototype (months 10–13) for rapid stakeholder communication, and a minimal hardware-token integration (months 14–16) using off-the-shelf FIDO2 keys and existing open-source smartcard stacks, with clear documentation of which approach aligns with different policy and procurement scenarios.
review: The choice between browser-based and hardware-token reflects a deeper question about who the fallback path is designed to protect against. A browser-based fallback protects against App Store removal or platform policy change, but not against OS-level credential theft or government pressure on operating-system vendors. A hardware-token fallback protects against both, but assumes citizens and public-sector staff will carry and use a second authentication device — a behavioral assumption not tested in the Danish context and not reflected in any of the three options.

name: Coalition targeting: civil society versus insider institutional access
decision_axis: This lever controls whether coalition-building emphasizes external pressure from civil-society and academic partners, or direct inside relationships with procurement officers, standards bodies, and MitID operator stakeholders.
consequences: An external coalition (privacy NGOs, security researchers, digital-rights groups) generates public narrative momentum and political cover for Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to act, but may be perceived as adversarial by incumbent operators and may lack standing in technical procurement discussions. An insider coalition (current and former Digitaliseringsstyrelsen staff, MitID technical advisors, EU eIDAS working-group members) gains direct access to decision-making but risks co-optation, insider skepticism about disruptive alternatives, and reputational entanglement with existing operators. A mixed coalition balances narrative power and insider credibility but requires careful sequencing and separate messaging.
options: Build a high-visibility external coalition anchored in Danish privacy organizations, international security research networks, and Folketinget digital-policy sympathizers, with public briefings and published position papers that create political cost for ignoring platform-dependency risk, deferring insider relationships to phase three.

Cultivate insider relationships with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen procurement teams, the MitID operator's technical staff, and Danish members of EU eIDAS working groups, positioning the project as a technical service to de-risk procurement rather than as external advocacy, keeping external coalition work minimal and delegated.

Maintain dual tracks: an external coalition of civil-society and academic partners that publishes feasibility findings and generates policy debate, paired with a separate insider engagement channel led by the regulatory/compliance specialist that frames platform-neutral fallback as a contingency requirement aligned with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's own 2026–2029 digital-sovereignty mandate.
review: Coalition composition reflects a bet about where real power to change procurement and architecture decisions resides. The external coalition assumes public and political pressure can shift Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's risk tolerance; the insider coalition assumes incumbent operators and procurement teams will optimize for resilience if technical evidence is credible. Neither option addresses the possibility that both external and insider actors may coordinate to preserve the status quo if platform-dependency is not perceived as a crisis before AltID lock-in occurs.

name: eIDAS Level alignment: Substantial versus higher assurance certification path
decision_axis: This lever controls whether fallback-authentication designs target eIDAS2 'Substantial' level certification or pursue higher-assurance ('High') level pathways with correspondingly stricter technical and governance requirements.
consequences: Targeting Substantial level allows broader architectural flexibility, faster certification pathways, and applicability across most public-sector services (tax, banking integration, healthcare portals). Pursuing High-level certification increases trust anchors and regulatory defensibility but requires stricter technical controls, third-party security evaluation, more rigorous audit trails, and longer certification timelines—potentially delaying demonstrator credibility and phase-three procurement leverage. The choice affects both technical scope and regulatory positioning: Substantial is pragmatic for rapid traction; High is strategically stronger but risks missing the AltID window if certification bodies are slow.
options: Design demonstrators and fallback pathways explicitly for eIDAS2 Substantial assurance level, document the architectural and governance choices that align with this level, and position Higher-level certification as a post-project evolution for production deployment, keeping phase-two and phase-three scope focused on securing Substantial-level language in AltID procurement documents.

Pursue High-level certification from the outset by engaging with Denmark's national eIDAS certification body (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or delegated authority) early in phase one, accepting slower demonstrator timelines but gaining regulatory clarity and a finished artifact that can directly influence AltID architecture without further certification risk.

Target Substantial for the primary FIDO2/OpenID Connect demonstrator to prove rapid technical feasibility, and separately explore what High-level certification would require for the hardware-token fallback, using the gap analysis as a policy argument that procurement should accept Substantial-level fallback access as acceptable for continuity scenarios while High-level alternatives mature.
review: Substantial versus High certification is framed as a scope-timing trade, but the real tension is whether Denmark's procurement bodies view fallback authentication as a commodity resilience feature (Substantial is sufficient) or as a high-assurance critical-infrastructure component (High-level required). None of the options directly probe this institutional preference, and the project risks investing in a certification pathway that procurement has already decided is unnecessary or, conversely, insufficient.

name: AltID engagement timing: early coalition-building versus late procurement insertion
decision_axis: This lever controls whether the project initiates formal dialogue with AltID leadership and procurement teams in phase one, or waits until phase two demonstrators are finished to present technical evidence and policy arguments.
consequences: Early engagement (phase one) risks premature exposure of incomplete thinking and potential dismissal before demonstrators prove feasibility, but establishes the project as a legitimate stakeholder and may shape AltID architecture during its design-phase flexibility window. Late engagement (phase two/three) maximizes demonstrator credibility and allows the project to present finished evidence, but encounters AltID procurement specifications that may already have locked in platform-dependent assumptions, reducing the project's leverage to modify architecture. Early timing trades defensibility for influence; late timing trades timing for technical confidence.
options: Initiate formal stakeholder meetings with AltID leadership and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen in month 2–3 of phase one, present the resilience and supplier-concentration risk framing, and request AltID's procurement and architecture timeline so the project can align demonstrator scope and phase-three advocacy to AltID's actual decision gates rather than assumed schedules.

Complete all phase-one feasibility work and obtain a published report and at least one formal written institutional response before any direct AltID engagement, ensuring the project approaches AltID in phase three with demonstrators, evidence, and external coalition backing rather than as an early-stage proposal.

Conduct preliminary scoping conversations with AltID technical leads in month 3–4 of phase one (framed as 'research coordination' rather than formal requirements engagement), gather information about AltID's architecture and certification assumptions, and use findings to sharpen phase-two demonstrator design without committing the project to an official advisory role until demonstrators are ready.
review: Early engagement assumes AltID procurement teams are open to input and that architecture decisions are genuinely flexible; late engagement assumes demonstrators and external pressure will override incumbent preferences and existing technical momentum. Neither option accounts for the likelihood that AltID's specifications may already be drafted or internally coordinated before the project's phase-one conclusions are published, making both early and late insertion ineffective if the window has already closed.

name: Phase-two demonstrator production environment: simulated versus lightweight-live integration
decision_axis: This lever controls whether demonstrators authenticate against a completely synthetic Danish IdP environment created for testing, or against a lightweight read-only interface to actual public-sector identity infrastructure.
consequences: A fully simulated environment ensures complete project autonomy and removes any risk of unintended interaction with live MitID systems or public-sector infrastructure, but limits the credibility of the authentication flow and may be dismissed as 'not representative of real-world constraints.' A lightweight read-only integration (e.g., querying public-sector identity attributes via a test gateway without triggering live authentication events) increases realism and demonstrates compatibility with actual infrastructure, but introduces regulatory, legal, and coordination complexity and may violate confidentiality assumptions unless formally approved. Simulation is legally and operationally safer; lightweight live integration is more credible but higher friction.
options: Develop a fully self-contained synthetic environment that mirrors Danish public-sector identity schema, authentication flows, and audit-logging requirements, using published eIDAS and NSIS specifications and industry-standard test data, and explicitly document how each element maps to real infrastructure so credibility does not depend on live integration.

Engage with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen early in phase one to negotiate a formal test-access agreement permitting read-only queries against a non-production identity-provider environment, use this controlled integration in the phase-two demonstrator, and document the access-control and audit-logging mechanisms as a proof that external actors can safely query public-sector identity attributes under governance constraints.

Create a hybrid approach: a primary demonstrator using a synthetic environment for full autonomy and fast iteration, paired with a secondary 'compatibility documentation' layer that shows how the demonstrator's API design maps to eIDAS and NSIS standards, allowing procurement reviewers to assess integration risk without requiring live infrastructure access.
review: Simulated versus live integration is presented as an autonomy-versus-credibility trade, but the real risk is that either approach may be perceived as insufficient by different institutional audiences. Procurement teams may dismiss simulation as 'not real'; Digitaliseringsstyrelsen may refuse test-environment access on security or legal grounds, forcing the project into pure simulation anyway. None of the options address the institutional mechanism for negotiating test access or the political cost of requesting it.

name: Phase-three outcome if AltID traction is weak: escalation pathway versus research consolidation pivot
decision_axis: This lever controls whether the project pursues formal escalation channels (e.g., Parlamentarisk Ombudsmand, Rigsrevisionen, EU bodies) if AltID procurement does not adopt platform-neutral requirements, or pivots toward research publication and EU standards consolidation as the primary delivery.
consequences: Escalation pathways (legislative audit, ombudsman inquiry, EU Digital Identity Taskforce escalation) apply external pressure and can force official response, but risk framing the project as adversarial and may trigger institutional resistance that hardens existing preferences. Research consolidation (peer-reviewed publication, EU standards working-group participation, long-form policy papers) establishes intellectual authority and ensures findings persist beyond AltID's current cycle, but delivers weaker short-term pressure on Danish procurement and may leave the 2026–2029 window uninfluenced. Escalation is high-stakes; consolidation is lower-impact but more durable.
options: If month-16 AltID engagement has not produced written acknowledgment of platform-neutrality or fallback-access requirements, initiate phase-three escalation: formal inquiry to Rigsrevisionen (Danish State Audit Office) on supplier-concentration risk in digital identity, briefings to relevant Folketinget committees, and EU-level escalation via the Digital Identity Taskforce, accepting reputational cost and potential institutional backlash.

Pivot phase three toward research publication and EU standards consolidation: publish demonstrators, feasibility report, and policy findings in peer-reviewed venues and EU working groups (eIDAS revision meetings, Digital Identity Wallet technical task forces), establish the project as an authoritative research voice on platform-neutral identity, and position findings for influence on post-2027 digital-identity revisions rather than AltID.

Maintain a dual-track contingency: if AltID is closed to influence by month 16, execute a partial escalation (Rigsrevisionen inquiry on supplier-concentration risk framed as a governance audit rather than a direct identity-architecture challenge) while simultaneously preparing high-impact publication and EU engagement, so the project applies pressure on multiple timescales without betting entirely on any single institutional pathway.
review: Escalation versus consolidation reflects a bet about whether institutional inertia or external pressure will dominate. Escalation assumes that formal audit or legislative pressure can override procurement momentum; consolidation assumes that intellectual authority and EU-level harmonization will matter more than Danish-level influence. The real weakness in all three options is that they are contingent on phase one and phase two not producing sufficient institutional traction—meaning the escalation decision is essentially pre-determined by phase-one coalition-building and AltID engagement strategy, but the project does not explicitly call this out in the gate criteria.

name: Demonstrator attestation model and trust-anchor exposure
decision_axis: This lever controls which trust anchors the demonstrator relies on by choosing between a fully self-signed proof-of-concept, a temporary test certificate from an existing Danish certification body, or a publicly recognized but non-production eIDAS-Substantial credential.
consequences: Choosing a self-signed or temporary certificate maximizes technical independence and speed but risks dismissal by procurement teams as 'not real authentication.' Conversely, securing a formal eIDAS-Substantial credential lends credibility but requires early engagement with certification bodies, lengthens phase two, and creates dependency on their timeline and approval criteria. The middle path—working with an existing Danish certification body on a scoped test credential—balances credibility and agility but may be unavailable if bodies view the project as politically sensitive.
options: Build the demonstrator on self-signed keys and rely entirely on transparent code review and academic security audit to establish feasibility without formal credential anchoring.
Engage a Danish or EU certification body early in phase two to issue a time-limited, non-production eIDAS-Substantial credential specifically for the fallback-authentication demonstrator.

Implement the demonstrator using a temporary test certificate from an internal or consortium-run certificate authority, published alongside code and architecture review to allow procurement teams to evaluate trust assumptions without formal certification overhead.
review: The core tension is between speed/independence and institutional credibility—procurement teams rarely accept 'credible research' without formal credentials, yet early certification engagement locks the project into external timelines. The options all assume the certification body or internal CA will cooperate; none addresses what happens if Denmark's designated eIDAS certification authorities refuse to issue test credentials for a project they perceive as undermining incumbent identity operators.

name: Fallback-authentication user experience: passive friction versus active choice
decision_axis: This lever controls how citizens encounter the fallback authentication path by choosing between embedding it as an automatic failover when primary MitID is unavailable, offering it as an opt-in alternative in settings, or routing it only to explicitly enrolled cohorts during a controlled rollout phase.
consequences: Automatic failover maximizes resilience and reduces conscious friction but risks consumer confusion if the fallback feels slow or unfamiliar, potentially driving users back to MitID and making the fallback appear useless. Opt-in discovery increases transparency and lets interested users test the path, but adoption remains low and procurement teams may argue the fallback is too niche to warrant architecture changes. Explicit cohort enrollment allows controlled measurement and builds evidence of adoption and satisfaction but requires defining and maintaining separate user segments, which contradicts the framing of platform-neutral access as universally available.
options: Implement the demonstrator to activate the fallback authentication path only when the primary MitID flow times out or returns an explicit platform-unavailability signal, with no user awareness or friction required.
Design the demonstrator interface to surface the fallback option prominently in advanced settings or accessibility panels, requiring users to actively select it if they wish to test or depend on it.

Restrict the initial phase-two demonstrator to a pre-enrolled pilot cohort (e.g., security researchers, civil-society organizations, and sympathetic procurers) and measure satisfaction and functionality outcomes before considering broader user-testing scope.
review: The core tension is between making fallback authentication 'just work' (which feels like undermining MitID's primary position) and making it so optional that no one uses it (which proves nothing about resilience). The options assume users will either notice or actively seek the fallback; none addresses the psychological dynamic that citizens prefer the convenience of their existing primary method, so even a frictionless fallback may appear irrelevant.

name: GrapheneOS versus alternative privacy-respecting platform selection
decision_axis: This lever controls which open-source or privacy-respecting Android-compatible operating system hosts the mobile demonstrator by choosing between committing to GrapheneOS as the baseline, supporting a portfolio approach across GrapheneOS and LineageOS or CalyxOS, or building on a generic AOSP fork to remain agnostic about specific hardened OS flavors.
consequences: Standardizing on GrapheneOS provides clear technical focus, simplifies security review, and aligns with modern hardening practices, but GrapheneOS is a small project with limited institutional support, making it a risky sole dependency and potentially alienating users or procurers who prefer other hardened platforms. A portfolio approach demonstrates broader compatibility and hedges against any single OS project's instability, but multiplies testing burden, increases phase-two costs, and diffuses technical focus. A generic AOSP fork avoids lock-in but sacrifices the security hardening and anti-tracking features that justify moving away from stock Android in the first place.
options: Commit the mobile demonstrator exclusively to GrapheneOS, documenting its hardening architecture and threat model thoroughly to justify why its specific privacy and integrity guarantees are essential to the fallback-authentication trust model.

Develop and test the demonstrator on GrapheneOS as the primary baseline, then systematically port and validate the authentication flow on LineageOS and CalyxOS to demonstrate that platform-neutral authentication does not require a single OS standard.

Build the demonstrator on a minimalist AOSP derivative that includes only essential security patches and no platform-specific telemetry, leaving space for integrators to layer their preferred hardening (GrapheneOS, CalyxOS, etc.) downstream.
review: The core tension is between standardizing on a mature, well-hardened platform (GrapheneOS) and avoiding the appearance of picking a niche OS that procurement teams may not support. None of the options addresses the practical reality that GrapheneOS has a tiny user base and no integration with Danish institutional Android deployment pipelines, so demonstrating it works there does not prove it will scale to mainstream procurement.

name: Standards and interoperability lever: OpenID Connect profile versus SAML versus EU eIDAS2 wallet protocol specifics
decision_axis: This lever controls which authentication protocol stack the demonstrator implements by choosing between a tightly scoped OpenID Connect discovery and profile optimized for Danish public-sector providers, a SAML 2.0 profile aligned with existing Nordic federations, or an early implementation of the emerging EU eIDAS2 Digital Identity Wallet protocol specification.
consequences: OpenID Connect is modern, widely adopted in the security and fintech sectors, and easier for web and mobile clients to integrate, but Danish public-sector infrastructure is historically SAML-heavy, so adoption friction in procurement is real. SAML 2.0 leverages existing Nordic identity federation infrastructure and reduces integration friction for incumbent operators, but perpetuates older protocol stacks and is seen by some standards bodies as legacy technology. Implementing eIDAS2 wallet protocols positions the project as forward-compatible with emerging EU mandates and differentiates it from MitID's legacy stack, but eIDAS2 specifications are still in flux, implementation guidance is sparse, and early adoption carries risk of rework if standards shift.
options: Implement the demonstrator using OpenID Connect with a custom claim profile optimized for Substantial-level authentication, Danish attributes (CPR number, organizational affiliations), and explicit no-telemetry or minimal-tracking requirements.

Build the demonstrator's authentication layer on SAML 2.0 assertions, integrating with existing Nordic identity federation metadata and attribute registries to prove that platform-neutral fallback access can operate within the federation ecosystem that already connects Danish public-sector services.

Prototype the demonstrator using an early draft or stable subset of the EU eIDAS2 Digital Identity Wallet protocol, working directly with relevant ETSI or CEN technical working groups to ensure the implementation influences specification maturity and signals Danish commitment to European interoperability standards.
review: The core tension is between adopting the most modern, technically superior protocol (eIDAS2) and choosing the path of least resistance with incumbent operators (SAML). None of the options acknowledges that Danish procurement may mandate whichever protocol MitID operators choose to support, making the demonstrator's protocol choice secondary to getting procurement language that forbids exclusive platform or protocol lock-in in the first place.

name: Coalition visibility and public narrative timing: early publication versus strategic surprise
decision_axis: This lever controls when and how the project's interim findings become public by choosing between publishing the phase-one feasibility report openly within four weeks of completion, embargoing key findings until phase-two demonstrators are complete, or selectively briefing Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, coalition members, and key EU stakeholders before any public launch.
consequences: Early publication maximizes credibility, activates civil-society attention, and creates political momentum, but risks alerting incumbent operators and procurement teams to counterargument earlier, potentially hardening institutional resistance before the project has demonstrated technical feasibility. Embargoed publication protects the technical narrative and gives demonstrators maximum credibility before public scrutiny, but delays coalition activation and may be perceived as secretive or evasive. Selective stakeholder briefing concentrates influence but risks leaks, inconsistent messaging, and accusations of backroom dealings if the project later appears to prioritize insider access over transparent advocacy.
options: Publish the phase-one feasibility and risk report through open-access channels (academic repository, project website, civil-society partners) immediately upon completion, scheduling concurrent public briefings and media engagement to frame the narrative before institutional critics can deploy counter-messaging.

Embargo public release of phase-one findings and delay open publication until phase-two demonstrators are functional and externally reviewed, using the interim period to brief Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and coalition partners under structured confidentiality agreements to build consensus before public discourse.

Conduct a targeted stakeholder briefing program in weeks 6–8 of phase one, presenting early findings to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, EU eIDAS working groups, and selected civil-society organizations, holding public release for the final feasibility report after gathering formal responses and addressing institutional concerns.
review: The core tension is between transparency-based legitimacy and insider-access influence—the project cannot simultaneously be a transparent advocacy vehicle and a quiet procurement-intelligence operation. None of the options acknowledges that once any key stakeholder is briefed, embargoes fail; the project must assume early findings will leak and plan messaging accordingly.

name: Budget contingency and phase-gating: milestone-based release versus proportional allocation versus hold-back reserve
decision_axis: This lever controls how the 10.5 million DKK is released across phases and contingencies by choosing between holding all phase-two and phase-three funding until phase-one milestones are formally verified, distributing 80 percent upfront and reserving 20 percent for contingency reallocation, or front-loading phase-one and phase-two with 70 percent and holding 30 percent until phase-three risks are clarified.
consequences: Strict milestone gating maximizes control and reduces waste but delays phase-two staffing and demonstrator work if phase-one verification takes longer than planned, potentially compressing the demonstrator timeline and reducing quality. Proportional allocation with a small reserve enables concurrent staffing and parallelized work but reduces flexibility if institutional blockages emerge, forcing difficult mid-project reallocation decisions. Front-loading with a large phase-three reserve maximizes demonstrator quality and technical credibility but leaves phase-three procurement and standards engagement under-resourced if phase-two slips, potentially squandering the technical work's political impact.
options: Release phase-one funding unconditionally as planned, but make phase-two funding contingent on publication of the feasibility report and at least one formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen; hold phase-three funding until month 16 and allocate based on traction assessment then.

Allocate 30 percent of total budget to phase one with immediate release, 50 percent to phase two with 50 percent released on phase-one completion and 50 percent on demonstrator functionality verification, and reserve 20 percent for phase-three reallocation or contingency staffing extension.

Front-load 60 percent of budget into phase one and phase two combined, with the full phase-two amount released at phase-one completion; reserve 40 percent for phase three, with internal checkpoints at months 16 and 20 to decide whether to pursue procurement-centered work or pivot to standards and research consolidation.
review: The core tension is between control and agility—strict gating protects finances but creates cliff-edge risks if milestones slip, while proportional allocation enables continuity but diffuses accountability for spending. None of the options accounts for the real-world likelihood that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen will request project modifications or delayed delivery in exchange for continued funding, making formal gating a weak control mechanism.

name: EU standards engagement scope: focused eIDAS2 Wallet working group versus horizontal ETSI liaison versus distributed bilateral diplomacy
decision_axis: This lever controls how the project participates in EU-level digital identity standardization by choosing between concentrating resources on the eIDAS2 Digital Identity Wallet technical working groups, establishing formal ETSI liaison and participating across multiple horizontal standards bodies, or dispersing engagement through bilateral briefings with key EU member states' digital identity authorities.
consequences: Focused eIDAS2 participation allows deep technical input, aligns with Danish procurement timelines, and concentrates team capacity, but risks isolation if the wider European discussion moves toward different architectural or interoperability models. Horizontal ETSI liaison broadens the project's influence and provides early signal of broader standards shifts, but requires dedicated standards-coordination staffing and exposes the project to resource dilution across competing workstreams. Bilateral diplomatic engagement builds peer-to-peer relationships with other Nordic and EU countries, potentially creating a coalition of platform-neutral advocates at the member-state level, but consumes significant travel and coordination effort with uncertain political return.
options: Assign a dedicated project member to participate actively in the eIDAS2 Digital Identity Wallet technical working group under ETSI/CEN, working to ensure that platform-neutrality, fallback-authentication, and resilience language is incorporated into emerging protocol and architecture guidelines.

Secure formal liaison status with ETSI and CEN, covering not just eIDAS2 wallet work but also relevant mobile security, authentication protocol, and interoperability standards streams, positioning the project as a source of pragmatic resilience and sovereignty input across multiple horizontal workstreams.

Conduct bilateral briefing engagements with digital identity authorities and procurement leads in Sweden, Norway, Finland, and key EU member states (e.g., Germany, Netherlands) to present the project's resilience and platform-neutrality case, aiming to build a coalition of member states advocating for similar requirements in EU-level guidance and procurement.
review: The core tension is between depth of technical influence (focused eIDAS2 work) and breadth of political alliance-building (horizontal and bilateral diplomacy). None of the options addresses the structural reality that EU standards-setting bodies are consensus-driven and slow-moving; even if the project influences language, translating that into binding procurement requirements at the member-state level remains a separate political problem.

name: Demonstrator failure escalation and narrative recovery: reframing versus research pivot versus delayed deployment
decision_axis: This lever controls how the project responds if the mobile or fallback-authentication demonstrator fails to authenticate correctly or is judged as insufficiently secure by phase-two reviewers by choosing between treating the failure as a technical refinement and committing to a working version by phase-three deployment, pivoting to a pure research and publishing track to extract lessons from the failure, or deferring demonstrator maturity goals and instead using phase three to focus on procurement language and standards pressure while acknowledging the technical path is longer.
consequences: Committing to demonstrator recovery by phase-three deployment maximizes the original theory of change but risks sinking remaining budget into rework while procurement windows close. Pivoting to research-and-publishing mode preserves intellectual credibility and produces academic output, but abandons the demonstrator-as-leverage strategy and concedes the original project goal. Deferring deployment and reframing the project's impact around procurement language alone hedges against technical failure but may appear to stakeholders as the project retreating from its core objective, potentially undermining institutional credibility.
options: If demonstrator authentication or security review uncovers blockers in phase two, commit to a second development iteration in phase three, with extended testing and external security validation, treating the deployed demonstrator as the primary evidence of feasibility rather than an interim research artifact.

If critical security or functionality issues emerge in phase-two testing that cannot be resolved within the remaining budget, pivot the project to document and publish the technical failures, architectural constraints, and lessons learned as an open-source research contribution, reframing success as helping the European digital identity community avoid similar dead-ends.

If phase-two demonstrator development reveals that production-equivalent fallback authentication requires substantially more work than budgeted, rebalance phase-three resources away from deployment readiness toward procurement-language advocacy and EU standards input, explicitly positioning the demonstrator as a research prototype rather than a production path.
review: The core tension is between technical ambition and political realism—if the demonstrator fails, the project's leverage over procurement becomes purely rhetorical, yet admitting this failure early enough to replan phase three requires courage and institutional transparency. None of the options addresses the likelihood that procurement teams will interpret any demonstrator failure as vindication that 'platform-neutral access is too hard' and therefore MitID's existing model is justified.
